contraventionDescription: Parked in a permit space without displaying a valid permit. You must not park in a permit holders only space during controlled hours without clearly displaying a valid permit – Contravention Code 16

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content:
1. An appropriate permit must be clearly displayed on the windscreen. If you have a valid parking permit which may have been obscured for example by frost on the windscreen, leaflets put under you windscreen wiper or has fallen off in damp weather, then send a copy of your permit with a representation and you should have your penalty charge notice cancelled.
Below we show diagrams of the signs and bay markings which must be in situ by law and much more information that will help you decide whether to appeal against your penalty charge notice.
2. If your parking permit has only recently expired then you should appeal on compassionate grounds, particularly if you are waiting for information from the DVLA, or indeed from the council itself which is required before a permit can be renewed. . A number of councils offer a zero tolerance policy so it is a good idea to put the expiry date of your permit in your diary.
3. Quite often motorists are confused by poor signage, for example where pay and display bays join meter bays. Some councils are particularly poor at signposting their various restrictions. If the signage is confusing you should take photographs and make representations. If the signage is not clear and does not comply then the contravention did not occur. Remember that there should be a clear and un-obscured sign within 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you parked. If you have different types of bays adjoining each other, for example pay and display bays adjoining residents parking bays then there should be a sign either within 5 meters of the point where the enforcement types meet (on each side of the demarcation line) or a sign at the point where the different enforcements meet with arrows pointing each way and clearly showing the different types of enforcement.  It is not sufficient for there to be just demarcation lines in the road.
4. Parking permit signs can only contain a maximum of 3 letters or numbers in addition to the enforcement hours and the words “permit holders only” otherwise they do not comply with the signage regulations unless special permission has been given by the department of transport. We have seen signs that do not comply for example some signs have four letters  “resident permit holders only “KA/CA” or similar letters.
5. Were the hours of enforcement clearly shown on the parking permit sign?
6. Are the resident’s bays enforced 24 hours a day? If so do the signs contain the words “At any time” on them. “At any time” is not an approved variation to the signs and a recent adjudicator’s decision went against a council on this point. You may well wish to appeal on this point. It is possible that the council have a specific permission for the use of these words which they would mention if your appeal is rejected. You can also check with the road signs policy unit at the department of transport.
7. Were the Road markings clear? A permit bay should be clearly shown by having broken white lines marking the restricted bays. They do not have to show individual bays but the lines must be visible. At the end of the bay(s) there should be single broken bay markings unless the bays adjoin pay and display bays. Many bays which, for example, adjoin yellow lines have double bay markings at the end which is non compliant.
8. Parking attendants can only issue one ticket per vehicle for the same offence in any 24 hour period.  However, don’t think you can stay there all day legally as your vehicle can be clamped or removed after the issue of a PCN.  Some motorists have received more than one PCN from the same Parking Attendant so on your appeal you may even have both  “PCNs” cancelled as a result of the attendant’s vexatious behaviour.
9. Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds in any appeal or representation.
Please see our section on parking adjudicators decisions which will have details of cases for this contravention.
 
Below: The required dimensions of an individually marked residents permit holders bay. Please note the permitted variants in the box below the diagram. Obviously if the bay isn’t to the correct dimensions then you may want to appeal. There are no guarantees but the bay should really be compliant in order for it to be enforceable.

 

Below: A diagram of the sign with the required dimensions. Again check the permitted variants and the sizes of the signs and the letters to see if the signs are compliant.
Back to Parking

Back to Parking
